best_for: Greenfield SaaS or marketing site shipping frontend+backend as one deployable; small team; Vercel-first hosting
avoid_when: Backend must scale independently of frontend, or a separate team owns backend release cycles
tradeoffs: Fastest path to a full-stack TypeScript app with shared types end-to-end; serverless cold starts can add latency to the first request after idle

best_for: Form submissions and mutations inside an existing Next.js App Router project, avoiding a separate API layer entirely
avoid_when: Public API consumed by non-Next.js clients (mobile app, third-party integrations)
tradeoffs: Removes API boilerplate and client-side fetch code, but couples the mutation to the framework — harder to reuse for a mobile client later

best_for: Team already knows Express; needs full manual control over middleware/routing; long-lived service with custom infra requirements
avoid_when: Team wants opinionated structure out of the box, or needs built-in dependency injection and testing scaffolding
tradeoffs: Minimal and flexible, huge ecosystem, but structure is 100% left to you — inconsistent codebases are common without team discipline

best_for: Larger team, enterprise-style backend needing DI, decorators, modular architecture, and built-in testing conventions
avoid_when: Small MVP or solo project where Nest's ceremony (modules/providers/decorators) slows initial velocity
tradeoffs: Angular-like structure imposes consistency across a team, first-class TypeScript and OpenAPI/Swagger generation, but steeper learning curve than Express

best_for: High-throughput API where raw request/response performance matters more than ecosystem size
avoid_when: Team wants Express's ubiquity/tutorials or Nest's structure
tradeoffs: Notably faster than Express under load with a similar plugin model, but a smaller ecosystem and fewer hires already know it

best_for: Python team building a typed, auto-documented REST/async API; data/ML-adjacent product; needs OpenAPI docs for free
avoid_when: Team has no Python expertise and no reason to introduce a second language into an otherwise all-JS stack
tradeoffs: Async-first, Pydantic validation gives you request/response typing and docs for free, excellent for data-heavy or ML-serving backends

best_for: Content-heavy or admin-heavy product that benefits from Django's batteries-included admin panel, ORM, and auth out of the box
avoid_when: Team wants async-first performance or a minimal footprint microservice
tradeoffs: Ships an admin UI, ORM, migrations, and auth almost for free; monolithic by default which is a feature for CRUD-heavy apps and friction for microservices

best_for: Same as Django, but the product is API-first (mobile app backend, SPA backend) rather than server-rendered pages
avoid_when: Team needs async performance DRF doesn't provide as naturally as FastAPI
tradeoffs: Mature serialization/permissions/viewset conventions on top of Django; less async-native than FastAPI

best_for: Postgres-backed product needing auth, storage, realtime, and row-level-security without standing up custom infrastructure; fastest path from zero to a real relational backend
avoid_when: Complex custom business logic that doesn't map well to Postgres functions/RLS, or a requirement to avoid vendor lock-in entirely
tradeoffs: Real Postgres (not a proprietary DB) means you can always self-host later; RLS policies double as your authorization layer, but non-trivial business logic still needs Edge Functions or an external service

best_for: Mobile-first product, needs offline-first sync (Firestore), and the team is already in the Google Cloud ecosystem
avoid_when: Relational data with complex joins/reporting needs — Firestore's NoSQL model fights you here
tradeoffs: Excellent offline sync and mobile SDKs, generous free tier, but NoSQL query limitations and can get expensive at scale (per-read pricing)

best_for: Custom server-side logic (webhooks, third-party API calls, cron jobs) alongside a Supabase-backed app, without leaving the Supabase project
avoid_when: Long-running or CPU-heavy jobs — Edge Functions are Deno-based serverless, not a general compute platform
tradeoffs: Deploys alongside your Supabase project with shared env/secrets; Deno runtime means most npm packages work but not all

best_for: Latency-sensitive, globally-distributed API; simple request/response logic; willing to work within Workers' constraints (no long-running processes, limited CPU time)
avoid_when: Backend needs a traditional filesystem, long-running background jobs, or heavy CPU-bound work
tradeoffs: Runs at the edge in every Cloudflare PoP for very low latency worldwide; pairs with D1 (SQLite) and KV for data, but the runtime is a V8 isolate, not full Node.js

best_for: Agency client site with a CMS-driven marketing site plus a handful of form/contact endpoints — no need for a standalone backend
avoid_when: Client requests a mobile app sharing the same backend, or expects the API to outlive the frontend rewrite
tradeoffs: Ships fast and cheap for client work, but the API is implicitly tied to the Next.js app's lifecycle/hosting — factor that into the contract/handoff

best_for: Agency building a multi-tenant SaaS for a client that will hand this off to an internal engineering team later
avoid_when: One-off client site with no ongoing engineering team to maintain the structure
tradeoffs: The imposed structure is easier for an unfamiliar team to pick up during handoff than a loosely-organized Express app

best_for: Agency delivering a client MVP fast, where the client wants to self-manage the backend afterward via a dashboard rather than hire a backend engineer
avoid_when: Client explicitly wants full infrastructure ownership/on-prem control
tradeoffs: Client gets a real Postgres dashboard (Table Editor, Auth, Storage) without needing to read code, at the cost of some platform lock-in to Supabase's tooling (though the underlying Postgres is portable)

best_for: Internal admin/ops tool for a data team that already writes Python for data pipelines and wants the backend in the same language
avoid_when: Team is JS/TS-only and would need to context-switch languages for a small internal tool
tradeoffs: Reuses existing Python expertise and libraries (pandas, etc.) directly in request handlers; smaller ecosystem of ready-made admin UI kits than Django

best_for: Internal admin tool where the built-in Django Admin can BE the entire UI, saving frontend build time
avoid_when: Tool needs a highly custom, branded UI beyond what Django Admin's templates support
tradeoffs: Django Admin can eliminate a frontend build entirely for pure CRUD internal tools — reassess if the tool later needs a public-facing polished UI

best_for: Real-time app (chat, live dashboard) using Socket.IO/ws on top of a familiar minimal framework
avoid_when: Team wants built-in structure for a large real-time system with many event types — consider Nest.js's Gateway abstraction instead
tradeoffs: Socket.IO integrates simply with Express; for many event types/rooms, Nest's WebSocket Gateways give more structure at scale

best_for: Real-time app that's mostly database-state-driven (live updates when rows change) rather than custom event logic
avoid_when: Real-time logic that isn't tied to database changes (e.g. ephemeral presence/cursor-sharing) — pair with a dedicated realtime service instead
tradeoffs: Postgres change-data-capture via Supabase Realtime removes the need to hand-wire a pub/sub layer for DB-driven updates

best_for: E-commerce storefront using Stripe/Shopify where checkout logic lives alongside the storefront frontend
avoid_when: High-volume order processing needing dedicated queueing/retry infrastructure beyond what serverless functions comfortably handle
tradeoffs: Simple to wire Stripe webhooks into Route Handlers; for high order volume, offload webhook processing to a queue rather than doing it inline

best_for: E-commerce/marketplace with complex catalog, inventory, and order-state logic that benefits from Django's ORM and admin for ops staff
avoid_when: Team needs the storefront itself to be highly interactive/SPA-like — pair Django (API mode via DRF) with a separate frontend rather than server-rendered templates
tradeoffs: Django Admin becomes the ops team's order-management console for free; DRF layered on top serves the storefront frontend

best_for: Mobile app backend needing push notifications, offline-first data, and quick auth (social login) without a custom server
avoid_when: Backend logic requires complex relational queries/reporting across large datasets
tradeoffs: Firebase Auth + Cloud Messaging + Firestore cover most mobile-app backend needs out of the box; complex reporting usually means exporting to BigQuery

best_for: Mobile app backend where the team wants a typed, testable API layer shared conceptually with a Next.js web app (same language, similar patterns)
avoid_when: Small mobile MVP where Nest's setup overhead isn't justified yet
tradeoffs: Consistent TypeScript across web+mobile backend reduces context-switching for a full-stack team

best_for: API that needs to run close to users across many geographic regions with minimal infra to manage (e.g. a public API product)
avoid_when: API needs persistent DB connections, long-running jobs, or more than a few dozen ms of CPU per request
tradeoffs: Near-zero cold starts and global edge placement; pairs naturally with Cloudflare D1/KV/R2 to stay within the platform rather than reaching out to a distant origin DB

best_for: Prototype/hackathon-speed MVP where the team needs to ship an API in hours, not days, and will likely rewrite it later anyway
avoid_when: Product is expected to scale past prototype without a planned rewrite — pick something with more structure up front instead
tradeoffs: Lowest ceremony option to get a working API fastest; technical debt is an accepted tradeoff at this stage

best_for: Prototype/hackathon MVP needing auth+DB+storage working in under an hour with zero backend code written
avoid_when: Prototype is purely a static frontend with no persistence needs at all
tradeoffs: Auth, Postgres, storage, and a REST/GraphQL API are auto-generated from the schema — probably the fastest zero-to-working-backend path available

best_for: Content-heavy site (blog/docs/marketing) that occasionally needs a dynamic endpoint (search, newsletter signup, contact form) but is mostly static/ISR
avoid_when: Content site needs a full CMS backend with complex editorial workflows — pair with a headless CMS instead of hand-rolling one
tradeoffs: Keeps the dynamic surface area small and colocated; for real editorial workflows, integrate a headless CMS rather than building content management by hand

best_for: Content-heavy site where non-technical editors need a real CMS-like admin experience and Django Admin can be customized to serve that
avoid_when: Content team needs a modern block-based editor UX — consider a dedicated headless CMS with a nicer editor UI instead
tradeoffs: Django Admin can be extended into a passable lightweight CMS for technical teams, but won't match a purpose-built headless CMS's editor UX

best_for: SaaS with multiple modules/domains (billing, users, notifications) that benefit from Nest's module system keeping boundaries explicit as the codebase grows
avoid_when: Single-domain app where multiple modules would be premature structure
tradeoffs: Enforces separation between domains from day one, which pays off as a SaaS grows past a handful of resources

best_for: SaaS with an AI/LLM feature (agents, RAG, embeddings) where the backend needs to call Python-native AI SDKs directly
avoid_when: Product has no AI/data component and would gain nothing from Python's ML ecosystem
tradeoffs: Native access to the Python AI/ML ecosystem (langchain, transformers, numpy, etc.) without a cross-language bridge

best_for: SaaS with an AI/LLM feature where the team wants to stream tokens directly to the React frontend using the same language end-to-end
avoid_when: Heavy Python-only AI tooling (fine-tuning, certain ML libraries) is required server-side
tradeoffs: Streaming responses via Route Handlers + the Vercel AI SDK is a well-trodden path; falls short if you need Python-specific ML tooling, in which case pair with a Python microservice

best_for: SaaS needing multi-tenant data isolation where Row-Level Security policies can enforce tenant boundaries at the database layer itself
avoid_when: Tenant isolation logic is too complex for RLS policies to express cleanly (e.g. highly dynamic permission trees) — enforce it in an application layer instead
tradeoffs: RLS makes tenant-scoped row visibility a database-enforced guarantee instead of an app-layer convention that can be forgotten in one endpoint

best_for: Internal tool/admin backend for a team that wants minimal dependencies and full control, without a framework's opinions getting in the way
avoid_when: Team would benefit from convention-over-configuration instead of deciding everything themselves for an internal tool
tradeoffs: Fewer decisions pre-made means faster to start for simple tools, slower to onboard a second engineer without documented conventions

best_for: Internal tool exposing a typed API consumed by a data science notebook or another internal Python service
avoid_when: Tool's only consumer is a browser frontend with no other Python-side consumers — the auto-docs benefit is smaller then
tradeoffs: Auto-generated OpenAPI schema makes the API self-describing for other internal tools/scripts to consume, a strong fit for internal Python ecosystems

best_for: Prototype needing real-time collaborative features (shared cursors, live document edits) shipped fast
avoid_when: Product needs strict relational integrity/complex transactions across documents
tradeoffs: Firestore's realtime listeners make collaborative prototypes fast to build; document model requires care to keep data consistent across writes

best_for: E-commerce storefront needing very fast global page/API response times, paired with Cloudflare D1 or an external DB via Hyperdrive
avoid_when: Checkout flow needs a traditional stateful server process (e.g. long webhook processing chains) — offload that piece elsewhere
tradeoffs: Workers + D1/Hyperdrive keeps the whole storefront stack on one low-latency edge platform; Hyperdrive lets Workers reach a normal Postgres/MySQL DB with pooling

best_for: SaaS API where you've measured that Express's overhead matters at your request volume and want a near-drop-in, faster alternative
avoid_when: Team has no performance data suggesting Express is actually the bottleneck — don't switch frameworks speculatively
tradeoffs: Meaningfully faster JSON serialization/routing than Express at high RPS; smaller plugin ecosystem means occasionally writing what Express would have had a plugin for

best_for: Mobile app backend where the team is Python-first and wants Django's ORM/migrations with a REST layer for the app to consume
avoid_when: Team needs GraphQL or wants async-native request handling
tradeoffs: DRF's serializers/permissions/viewsets are mature and well-documented for mobile API backends; less async-friendly than FastAPI for high-concurrency streaming use cases

best_for: Agency delivering multiple small client projects that each need auth+DB+storage, where reusing one battle-tested pattern across clients saves time
avoid_when: A specific client contractually requires a different cloud vendor (AWS-only shop, etc.)
tradeoffs: One consistent stack across client projects speeds up delivery and makes handoff docs reusable, at the cost of being locked into Supabase's specific tooling choices per project

best_for: Greenfield SaaS defaulting choice when no strong constraint points elsewhere — the pragmatic starting point for most new full-stack web products in 2026
avoid_when: Any of the avoid_when conditions on rows BE001/BE002/BE009 apply — reassess against those first
tradeoffs: This is the skill's default recommendation absent other signals (see Stack Selection Logic in SKILL.md): Next.js + Supabase covers the majority of MVP/SaaS/agency-site cases with the least new infrastructure to learn
